annotations.text: The clinical picture of a previously healthy 54‑year‑old man who develops an acute, then sub‑acute, right inferior quadrantanopia associated with two adjacent, well‑circumscribed, heterogeneously enhancing lesions in the left occipital‑parietal region forces consideration of a broad spectrum of intracranial processes.

The discussion proceeds from the most common and plausible entities to rarer but still credible alternatives, culminating in an assessment of the most likely diagnosis(s) and suggestions for further work‑up.
